description:
You are an infrastructure engineer who has just logged in to a brand-new Linux host (the container you are in right now).  
Your first step in automating future provisioning tasks is to put a small, well-defined directory skeleton in place under your home directory, populate it with a few starter files, and generate a very small log of what you did.

Target layout and file contents
--------------------------------
When you are finished, the filesystem must contain **exactly** the following new directories and files (nothing more, nothing less):

1. Directory  : /home/user/provisioning  
2. Sub-dir    : /home/user/provisioning/terraform  
3. Sub-dir    : /home/user/provisioning/ansible  
4. Sub-dir    : /home/user/provisioning/logs  

5. File       : /home/user/provisioning/README.md  
   Required content (3 lines, each ending with a newline):  
   Line 1: # Provisioning skeleton  
   Line 2: This tree was created automatically for future infrastructure code.  
   Line 3: — The DevOps team  

6. File       : /home/user/provisioning/terraform/providers.tf  
   Required content (4 lines):  
   Line 1: terraform {  
   Line 2:   required_version = ">= 1.0"  
   Line 3: }  
   Line 4: # provider stubs will be added later  

7. File       : /home/user/provisioning/ansible/hosts.ini  
   Required content (3 lines):  
   Line 1: [web]  
   Line 2: web01 ansible_host=192.168.56.10  
   Line 3: web02 ansible_host=192.168.56.11  

8. File       : /home/user/provisioning/logs/bootstrap.log  
   This is the **verification file**.  
   It must contain exactly four lines (each ending in `\n`) that describe the directory creation actions in the order they happened, with no extra spaces:  

        CREATE_DIR:/home/user/provisioning
        CREATE_DIR:/home/user/provisioning/terraform
        CREATE_DIR:/home/user/provisioning/ansible
        CREATE_DIR:/home/user/provisioning/logs

Formatting rules for bootstrap.log:  
• One line per action, no leading or trailing whitespace.  
• No timestamp, no additional text.  
• Case-sensitive: use upper-case `CREATE_DIR:` exactly as shown.

Execution expectations
----------------------
• Work strictly inside your own home directory; you do **not** need sudo.  
• Use as few commands as you like, but they will naturally amount to about 3-4 shell commands (e.g. `mkdir -p`, `echo "…" >`, etc.).  
• The test harness will open and read the files verbatim, so pay close attention to spelling, punctuation, capitalisation and newlines.  
• Do **not** leave any stray files or folders anywhere else on the filesystem.

A quick mental checklist before you finish:
- All four directories exist.  
- All three content files have the exact text shown above.  
- The log file exists at the exact path, has four lines, and each line matches the prescribed `CREATE_DIR:<path>` format.  
- No other artefacts were created.

Once the above conditions are met, the automated tests will pass.
truth:
Expected artifacts for verification:

Directories:
1. /home/user/provisioning
2. /home/user/provisioning/terraform
3. /home/user/provisioning/ansible
4. /home/user/provisioning/logs

Files and their exact byte contents (every line terminated by \n):

/home/user/provisioning/README.md
---------------------------------
# Provisioning skeleton
This tree was created automatically for future infrastructure code.
— The DevOps team
\n  (i.e. file ends with a newline after the third line)

/home/user/provisioning/terraform/providers.tf
----------------------------------------------
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.0"
}
# provider stubs will be added later
\n

/home/user/provisioning/ansible/hosts.ini
----------------------------------------
[web]
web01 ansible_host=192.168.56.10
web02 ansible_host=192.168.56.11
\n

/home/user/provisioning/logs/bootstrap.log
-----------------------------------------
CREATE_DIR:/home/user/provisioning
CREATE_DIR:/home/user/provisioning/terraform
CREATE_DIR:/home/user/provisioning/ansible
CREATE_DIR:/home/user/provisioning/logs
\n

No additional files or directories should be present under /home/user/provisioning or elsewhere.

Permissions:
All directories: 755
All files: 644
